names.japanese_name: 酵素処理カルボキシメチルセルロース

natural_occurrence.description: Enzymatically hydrolyzed carboxymethyl cellulose is a semi-synthetic derivative of cellulose. It's created by chemically modifying plant cellulose with carboxymethyl groups, then enzymatically breaking down the polymer chains into shorter fragments. It's processed wood pulp or cotton that has been chemically and enzymatically modified.

manufacturing.description: Produced by first creating sodium carboxymethylcellulose (E466) through chemical modification of cellulose, then treating it with cellulase enzymes to partially break down the polymer chains. This enzymatic hydrolysis creates shorter chain fragments with lower viscosity than regular CMC but retains the beneficial emulsifying and stabilizing properties. The degree of hydrolysis can be controlled to achieve desired viscosity.

other_uses.cosmetics: Low-viscosity stabilizer in liquid cosmetics and personal care products.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Excipient in liquid formulations, low-viscosity binder.

eu.conditions: Approved for use in specified food categories; less commonly used than regular CMC (E466).

japan.japanese_regulation_name: 酵素分解カルボキシメチルセルロース

commonly_found_in: Some processed foods as low-viscosity stabilizer
Clear beverages (where high viscosity would be undesirable)
Less commonly encountered than regular CMC (E466)
fun_facts: Enzymatically hydrolyzed CMC is essentially pre-digested sawdust - enzymes break down the chemically modified wood fiber into smaller pieces
The enzymatic treatment makes it less viscous (thinner) than regular CMC, perfect for beverages where you want stabilization without thickness
It's like the difference between long spaghetti and broken spaghetti pieces - same material, but shorter chains behave differently
Completely indigestible by humans (we lack the right enzymes), so it passes through as dietary fiber with zero calories
The enzymes used to break it down are the same type that termites and fungi use to digest wood cellulose in nature
